title: How China Is Handling AI’s Risk To Jobs
text:
How China Is Handling AI’s Risk To Jobs

Andrew Batson

Gavekal Dragonomics | 22 Jul 2026

China’s government is all-in on the artificial-intelligence revolution. As top leader Xi Jinping put it in a speech last week, AI is “a new engine of world economic growth and an accelerator of the transition from old to new growth drivers.” It’s not surprising, therefore, that the doom-mongering around the impact of artificial intelligence on employment so prevalent in the US has had little influence on official rhetoric and policy in China. The government sees no need to panic, and is not preparing for scenarios in which AI wipes out half of entry-level white-collar jobs or pushes unemployment above 10%.Analytically, that has been the correct stance: the predicted labor-market disruption in the US, where use of large language models has spread faster and wider than in China, has so far failed to materialize. Most research finds no aggregate impact of AI on US labor-market trends, though there is concern about demand for entry-level workers and software engineers. The stability could be because companies simply haven’t yet figured out how to replace workers with AI, or because AI in practice is more of a complement to than a replacement for skilled workers, or because higher productivity from AI ends up raising demand for workers’ output. AI has not yet brought a labor-market apocalypse, and perhaps it never will.
